title: Cat Drooling After Licking a Plant: Causes and Urgency Guide
meta_desc: Cat drooling after licking a plant? Learn about oral irritation, the medium urgency level, and why a photo of the plant is vital for triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Drooling after plant contact is usually a reaction to oral irritants, but identifying the plant is vital to rule out systemic poisoning. Monitor for symptoms like vomiting or lethargy and contact a vet if you are unsure of the plant's toxicity.

Why is my cat drooling after biting or licking a plant?

When a cat licks or bites a plant and begins to drool excessively (ptyalism), it is typically a reaction to chemical irritants or toxins within the plant's tissues. Many common household plants contain insoluble calcium oxalate crystals that cause an immediate stinging sensation, oral swelling, and increased saliva production upon contact.

Is my cat's drooling after eating a plant considered an emergency?

  • The urgency level for this situation is Medium.
  • While some plants only cause temporary local irritation, others can lead to systemic poisoning or severe airway swelling.
  • Monitor your cat closely for additional signs of distress.
  • If the drooling is accompanied by vomiting, difficulty swallowing, or lethargy, contact a veterinarian or a pet poison control hotline immediately.

How does taking a photo of the plant help a veterinarian triage my cat?

  • Identifying the specific plant species is the most critical step in determining the risk level.
  • Taking a clear photo of the plant (and any bite marks on it) allows a veterinary professional to quickly identify the toxin.
  • A photo helps the vet decide whether your cat needs an immediate office visit or if the symptoms can be managed with home care.
  • Home care steps may include rinsing the mouth with water or a small amount of tuna juice to soothe irritation.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Plant ingestion in cats can lead to drooling (excess salivation) due to oral mucosa irritation and edema caused by calcium oxalate crystals, saponins, or other irritants found in various plant parts including leaves, seeds, and sap. Clinical signs range from immediate pain and inflammation in the mouth and throat to anorexia, vomiting, diarrhea, and dyspnea. Treatment focuses on aggressive supportive and symptomatic therapy including rehydration, electrolyte replacement, analgesics, and demulcents as indicated, with extended monitoring based on exposure dose and severity of clinical signs.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3105)

faqs.question: What are common plants that cause drooling?
faqs.answer: Plants like Peace Lilies, Pothos, Philodendrons, and Dieffenbachia are common culprits because they contain sharp calcium oxalate crystals.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No, never induce vomiting in a cat unless specifically instructed to do so by a veterinarian, as this can cause further damage to the esophagus.

faqs.question: How long will the drooling last?
faqs.answer: If it is a simple irritant, drooling usually subsides within a few hours once the mouth is rinsed and the irritation fades.
